text.title: Story Mapping Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Five failure modes that produce maps which mislead release planning or break under execution.

antipattern.description: Backbone activities written as nouns ("Dashboard", "Reports") instead of verb phrases ("View Dashboard", "Generate Report").
antipattern.reason: Nouns hide the user action and make priority ordering arbitrary. Verb phrases are testable: you can verify whether a user can accomplish the activity.

antipattern.description: Walking skeleton skips one or more backbone activities.
antipattern.reason: A skeleton with gaps means the user cannot complete the end-to-end journey. Releases built on a broken skeleton ship an incomplete experience by design.

antipattern.description: Release slice covers only 1–2 activities out of the full backbone.
antipattern.reason: Users hit a wall at the edge of the covered activities and cannot complete their goal. Every release must span the full backbone — even if each column gets only one task in that release.

antipattern.description: Map built once at project start and never updated.
antipattern.reason: A static map loses fidelity within one sprint cycle as scope changes. Treat it as a living artifact tied to quarterly roadmap reviews.

antipattern.description: Only happy-path tasks in each activity column.
antipattern.reason: Error paths, empty states, and failure recovery are where users abandon products. Missing them from the map means they are never prioritized into a release.
